notes: lead_weeks = weeks before wedding date task should be COMPLETE. Negative = weeks after. due_date = wedding_date - lead_weeks*7 days. Pinned dates override. wedding_types: which wedding kinds the task applies to (uk = ceremony in UK, destination = wedding abroad); onboarding asks which type, engine filters accordingly.

tasks.title: Celebrate your engagement
tasks.guidance: Before any planning: enjoy it. Tell family and close friends first, then wider circles. No decisions needed this week.

tasks.title: Set your overall budget
tasks.guidance: Agree the total you can spend and who is contributing (you, parents, savings plan). The UK average is ~£20,600 but a quarter of couples spend under £10k. Every later decision hangs off this number.

tasks.title: Estimate your guest numbers
tasks.guidance: A rough day/evening split is enough. Guest count drives venue size and catering, which is 40-50% of most budgets. Decide early: adults only or children too?

tasks.title: Choose your wedding style and vision
tasks.guidance: Rustic barn, city hotel, festival field, classic church? Pick 3 words that describe the day you want. This keeps every supplier decision consistent later.

tasks.title: Shortlist a season or target date
tasks.guidance: Hold a season and 2-3 candidate dates rather than one fixed date - venue availability will decide the final one. Peak (May-Sep Saturdays) costs 20-40% more than off-peak.

tasks.title: Set up a planning hub
tasks.guidance: A shared email address for supplier enquiries and one place for quotes and contracts keeps two planners in sync and your personal inbox sane.

tasks.title: Research venue types and areas
tasks.guidance: Barns, hotels, stately homes, registry office plus restaurant, marquee at home. Filter by capacity, area and budget before falling in love with anywhere. Venue plus catering averages £9-11k in the UK.

tasks.title: Draft your guest list
tasks.guidance: Do it as households, with day/evening flags. The classic method: each partner lists must-haves, then merge, then trim together. Expect 10-20% declines.

tasks.title: Choose your destination country
tasks.guidance: Weigh flight cost/time for guests, weather in your target month (avoid hurricane/monsoon seasons), and how complex the country's marriage laws are. Expect 30-50% guest declines for weddings abroad.

tasks.title: Decide your ceremony type
tasks.guidance: Civil ceremony (register office or approved venue), Church of England, other religious, or celebrant-led (with a separate legal signing). This determines the whole legal pathway.

tasks.title: Decide: legal ceremony abroad or legal at home
tasks.guidance: Many couples do the legal paperwork quietly at a UK register office, then hold the full celebration abroad - it sidesteps foreign bureaucracy entirely. If you marry legally abroad, budget 3-4 months for documents.

tasks.title: Shortlist venues and book viewings
tasks.guidance: Shortlist 3-5 and view them within a couple of weeks so comparisons are fresh. Take photos and notes - they blur together fast.

tasks.title: Attend venue viewings
tasks.guidance: View at the time of day you'd marry. Check the wet-weather plan, accessibility for older guests, and where photos would happen.
ask_suppliers: What exactly is included in the price?
Is the venue exclusively ours?
What's the wet-weather plan?
Are there noise restrictions or a music curfew?
Corkage and minimum numbers?
Which registrars do you work with?

tasks.title: Contact the register office or church
tasks.guidance: For civil ceremonies, check registrar availability for your date BEFORE confirming the venue - popular dates book out. For CofE, meet the vicar to confirm eligibility and date.

tasks.title: Research the country's marriage requirements
tasks.guidance: Start at GOV.UK 'Getting married abroad' for your country. Common requirements: CNI or statutory declaration, residency periods (France requires 30+ days!), translated documents, local witnesses. Rules vary hugely by country.

tasks.title: Choose your wedding party
tasks.guidance: Best man, bridesmaids, ushers, flower girls. Ask people you can rely on, not just oldest friends - they'll have real jobs on the day.

tasks.title: Book your venue and confirm the date
tasks.guidance: The keystone booking - everything else dates from this. Read the contract before paying the deposit: cancellation terms, what's included, minimum numbers, payment schedule.
ask_suppliers: What deposit holds the date and is it refundable?
When are staged payments due?
What happens if we need to change the date?

tasks.title: Book the registrar or officiant
tasks.guidance: Registrar attendance at an approved venue typically costs £400-£600, booked through the local council. Celebrants are £600-£1,000 plus a separate register-office legal signing.
ask_suppliers: What times are available on our date?
What's your cancellation policy?
Can we personalise the ceremony wording?

tasks.title: Review and file the venue contract
tasks.guidance: Check cancellation and postponement clauses, service charge, and exactly what's included (chairs, linen, staffing). Query anything vague in writing before signing.

tasks.title: Take out wedding insurance
tasks.guidance: Buy before you pay any large deposits - it covers supplier failure, cancellation and illness. Cheap protection for a £20k event.
ask_suppliers: What's covered if a supplier goes bust?
Is COVID-style disruption covered?
What's the single-item limit?

tasks.title: Research wedding outfit styles
tasks.guidance: Save looks you love, then book boutique appointments. Go in with an open mind - most people buy something different from their Pinterest board.

tasks.title: Research photographers
tasks.guidance: Style first (documentary, classic, editorial), then availability, then price. Look at FULL galleries of real weddings, not just portfolio highlights - and check they've shot your venue's lighting conditions.

tasks.title: Hire a local wedding planner or coordinator
tasks.guidance: Near-essential abroad: they know the legal process, speak the language, and vet local suppliers you can't visit. Many resort venues include one.
ask_suppliers: Have you handled UK couples' legal paperwork before?
Which local suppliers do you work with?
How do payments work (currency, schedule)?
Will you attend on the day?

tasks.title: Book the venue abroad
tasks.guidance: If you can't visit first, insist on a live video walkthrough and recent real-wedding photos. Check what happens to your deposit if travel disruption forces a date change.
ask_suppliers: What's included for destination packages?
What's your postponement policy for travel disruption?
Can you handle the legal ceremony logistics locally?

tasks.title: Check insurance covers a wedding abroad
tasks.guidance: Standard UK wedding insurance often excludes or limits overseas weddings - you may need a specific destination policy plus travel insurance for both of you. Check supplier-failure cover applies to foreign suppliers.

tasks.title: Collect guest addresses and contacts
tasks.guidance: Chase these before save-the-dates. A shared link guests fill in themselves saves weeks of texting (Confetti gives you one).

tasks.title: Book bridal boutique appointments
tasks.guidance: Weekend slots book weeks ahead. Take max 2-3 trusted people - a big audience makes the decision harder.

tasks.title: Decide your catering approach
tasks.guidance: Many venues include or mandate in-house catering - check before researchingexternal  caterers. Dry-hire venues need external caterers booked much earlier.

tasks.title: Book the photographer
tasks.guidance: Good photographers book 12-18 months ahead for peak dates. This is the supplier couples most regret cutting corners on - photos are what survives the day.
ask_suppliers: How many hours and images are included?
Who shoots if you're ill?
When do we get the gallery?
Do you have backup equipment and insurance?
Print rights included?

tasks.title: Choose your stationery style and supplier
tasks.guidance: Decide the route: stationer, online print (cheaper), or DIY. One consistent suite across save-the-dates, invitations and on-the-day items looks far more polished.
ask_suppliers: What's the lead time per piece?
Do you offer a full matching suite?
Cost per extra invitation later?

tasks.title: Order the wedding dress
tasks.guidance: Made-to-order dresses take 6-9 months plus alterations, hence the early deadline. Off-the-rack, sample sales and pre-loved are great faster/cheaper routes.
ask_suppliers: What's the delivery lead time?
Are alterations included or extra?
What deposit and payment terms?

tasks.title: Research and shortlist caterers
tasks.guidance: Only needed for dry-hire venues. Compare like-for-like: per-head price including staff, equipment, linen and VAT - quotes hide different things.
ask_suppliers: What exactly does the per-head price include?
Who provides tableware and linen?
How do you handle dietary requirements?
Is a tasting included?

tasks.title: Book a videographer
tasks.guidance: The most common 'wish we had' regret. Even a highlights-only package captures voices - the thing photos can't.
ask_suppliers: Highlights film length and full-ceremony option?
How do you record audio for vows and speeches?
Drone footage - and is the venue OK with it?

tasks.title: Send save-the-dates extra early
tasks.guidance: 12+ months' notice for weddings abroad - guests need to book leave and save for flights. Include destination, dates and rough cost expectations so people can decide early.

tasks.title: Order save-the-dates
tasks.guidance: Or send digital ones free - guests only need the date and rough location. Spend the money on invitations instead if the budget is tight.

tasks.title: Send save-the-dates
tasks.guidance: Send 9-12 months out (longer for destination weddings). Day guests only - evening guests just get invitations later.

tasks.title: Book the caterer
tasks.guidance: UK average is £70-£130 per head for a three-course wedding breakfast including staff. Confirm the price-per-head lock: some contracts allow increases before the day.

tasks.title: Research florists and floral styles
tasks.guidance: Seasonal and local dramatically cuts cost - a May wedding full of peonies works; a December one doesn't. Save examples with rough prices attached.

tasks.title: Decide your entertainment
tasks.guidance: Band, DJ, or band-then-DJ. Check your venue's sound limiter and curfew FIRST - a 90dB limiter rules out many live bands.

tasks.title: Discuss honeymoon ideas and budget
tasks.guidance: Straight after the wedding or a 'mini-moon' now and the big trip later? The average UK couple spends £3,500-£5,000.

tasks.title: Book the band or DJ
tasks.guidance: Watch live or video footage before booking. Confirm set times, breaks (who plays music in them?), and space/power needs with the venue.
ask_suppliers: How many sets and how long?
What happens in breaks?
Do you have PAT certificates and PLI insurance (venues require both)?
Can we pick must-play and do-not-play lists?

tasks.title: Create a supplier payment schedule
tasks.guidance: List every deposit and balance with its due date. Most balances land 2-6 weeks before the day - couples get caught by the pile-up. Confetti tracks these as tasks.

tasks.title: Decide suits: hire or buy
tasks.guidance: Hire (~£100-£150 each) suits matching groups; buying (£200-£500+) makes sense for something you'll rewear. Decide who pays for the wedding party's outfits.

tasks.title: Book the florist
tasks.guidance: Brief them on venue, colours and budget - a good florist designs to a number. Bouquets, buttonholes, ceremony and table flowers is the standard package.
ask_suppliers: What's achievable within our budget?
Do you set up and take down at the venue?
Can ceremony flowers be moved to the reception?
Delivery timings on the day?

tasks.title: Book time off work
tasks.guidance: Book the wedding week AND the honeymoon, plus 1-2 days before the wedding for final jobs. Do it before colleagues take the same summer slots.

tasks.title: Share guest travel and booking options
tasks.guidance: A group booking or recommended flights/hotels list with price bands helps guests commit. Consider a room block at the venue and cheaper nearby options - guests are paying to attend.

tasks.title: Decide which suppliers travel with you
tasks.guidance: Flying your UK photographer out (flights + 2 nights) often costs similar to booking blind locally - and you've met them. Hair/makeup and flowers are usually better sourced locally via your planner.

tasks.title: Order wedding-party outfits
tasks.guidance: Bridesmaids' dresses have long lead times too. High-street multiway dresses in one colour are the budget-friendly crowd-pleaser.

tasks.title: Book ceremony and reception musicians
tasks.guidance: A string duo or acoustic guitarist for the ceremony and drinks reception adds a lot of atmosphere per pound.

tasks.title: Research hair and makeup artists
tasks.guidance: Look for artists whose portfolio matches YOUR hair type and skin tone. On-the-day bookings cover you plus however many of the wedding party you're treating.

tasks.title: Book the honeymoon
tasks.guidance: Mention it's your honeymoon everywhere - upgrades happen. Booking flights+hotel as a package adds ATOL protection.

tasks.title: Book your own flights and accommodation
tasks.guidance: Book in your CURRENT legal names (passports won't show a new name yet). Arrive at least 2-3 days early - some countries require it legally, and it buffers lost luggage and jet lag.

tasks.title: Book hair and makeup
tasks.guidance: Popular artists book a year out for summer Saturdays. Confirm how many people they can do in the morning and what time they'd need to start.
ask_suppliers: Is a trial included?
How long per person on the morning?
Do you stay for touch-ups?
Travel fees?

tasks.title: Plan wedding-day transport
tasks.guidance: Map every journey: to the ceremony, ceremony to reception (if separate), and how you leave at night. Don't forget the wedding party's journeys.

tasks.title: Book wedding-night accommodation
tasks.guidance: If your venue doesn't include a suite, book somewhere close now - wedding dates sell out local hotels.

tasks.title: Arrange guest accommodation options
tasks.guidance: Negotiate a group rate at 1-2 hotels near the venue and share options on your invitations or wedding info page. Guests book their own.
ask_suppliers: Can you hold a block of rooms without deposit?
What's the release date for unbooked rooms?

tasks.title: Engagement shoot
tasks.guidance: Often bundled free. Worth doing - you'll learn how to relax in front of their camera before the day.

tasks.title: Book a UK legal ceremony (if celebrating abroad)
tasks.guidance: If you chose the legal-at-home route: a simple register office ceremony with two witnesses costs ~£57 plus notice fees. Some couples keep it secret; others make it a small family lunch.

tasks.title: Order or reserve the suits
tasks.guidance: Get everyone measured - remote groomsmen can be measured at any tailor and send numbers. Made-to-measure needs 8-12 weeks.
ask_suppliers: When do hired suits arrive and go back?
What's the fit-issue policy days before?
Are accessories (ties, pocket squares) included?

tasks.title: Research wedding rings
tasks.guidance: Metal should generally match the engagement ring. Try shaped bands if the engagement ring has a low-set stone.

tasks.title: Book wedding cars
tasks.guidance: Classic cars book earliest. Check the car fits the dress. A well-valeted family car with ribbons is the zero-regret budget option.
ask_suppliers: How many trips are included?
What's the wet-weather plan for open-tops?
What happens if the car breaks down?

tasks.title: Finalise the guest list
tasks.guidance: Lock the day/evening split, plus-ones policy, and children policy before invitations print. Changing after invitations is where family friction starts.

tasks.title: Choose accessories
tasks.guidance: Shoes, veil, jewellery, hair pieces. Buy shoes before the first fitting - hem length is set to them.

tasks.title: Order the wedding cake
tasks.guidance: Good cake makers book 6+ months out. A smaller display cake plus kitchen sheet cakes feeds the same crowd for less.
ask_suppliers: Do you deliver and set up?
How is it stored and cut - who does that?
Cost per extra tier?

tasks.title: Plan décor and styling
tasks.guidance: Lighting changes a room more than anything else per pound - festoon, candles (check venue policy), uplighters. Decide DIY vs a stylist.

tasks.title: Order invitations
tasks.guidance: Order 10% extra for mistakes and keepsakes. Proofread names, date, times and postcode twice - reprints cost more than the extras.

tasks.title: Decide on a gift list or registry
tasks.guidance: Gift list, honeymoon fund, charity donations or nothing - decide before invitations go out so you can include the details.

tasks.title: Choose your menu
tasks.guidance: Pick crowd-pleasers over adventure - you're feeding 80 people with one menu. One veggie/vegan option and a kids' menu covers most needs.

tasks.title: Plan the drinks package
tasks.guidance: Typical formula: one drink on arrival, half a bottle of wine with the meal, a toast drink, then paid bar. Check corkage (£10-£20/bottle) if you want to supply your own.
ask_suppliers: What's the corkage charge?
Can we do a tab-then-paid bar?
Are soft drinks included for kids and drivers?

tasks.title: Start a skincare and grooming routine
tasks.guidance: Nothing drastic near the day: no new products in the final month, no first-time facial the week before, haircuts 1-2 weeks out so they settle.

tasks.title: Brief the hen and stag organisers
tasks.guidance: Your job is only: dates you can do, budget guidance for guests, and a veto list. Then let them plan. Schedule at least 3-4 weeks before the wedding, never the weekend before.

tasks.title: Arrange guest transport
tasks.guidance: A coach between ceremony, reception and town at the end of the night is money well spent for rural venues - it gets guests home and the bar takings up.

tasks.title: Check passports and visas
tasks.guidance: Many countries require 6 months' validity beyond your travel dates. NOTE: you cannot travel under a new married name until your passport is changed - book tickets in your current legal name.

tasks.title: Obtain your Certificate of No Impediment (CNI)
tasks.guidance: For legal ceremonies in most non-Commonwealth countries: give notice at your local register office (~£42), notice is displayed 28 days, then the CNI is issued. Most countries require it issued within 3 months of the wedding - so don't do it too early either. Commonwealth destinations (Cyprus, Mauritius, Barbados...) use a statutory declaration from a solicitor instead.

tasks.title: Attend the menu tasting
tasks.guidance: Usually included once numbers are confirmed. Taste wines alongside the food, and speak up about tweaks - good kitchens expect it.

tasks.title: Choose ceremony readings and music
tasks.guidance: Civil ceremonies can't include religious content - your registrar will approve choices in advance. Usually 2 readings and 3-4 pieces of music (entrance, signing, exit).

tasks.title: Send invitations
tasks.guidance: Send 3-4 months before with an RSVP deadline 6-8 weeks before the day. Include accommodation options, gift list info, dress code and your RSVP link.

tasks.title: Buy the wedding rings
tasks.guidance: Allow 4-6 weeks for sizing and engraving. Keep the boxes - you'll hand them to the best man on the morning.
ask_suppliers: Is resizing free later?
Engraving cost and lead time?
Is the metal hallmarked?

tasks.title: Book a photo booth
tasks.guidance: Reliable evening entertainment and doubles as a guest book. Check it includes an attendant and unlimited prints.

tasks.title: Hire décor items
tasks.guidance: Arches, candle holders, table runners, signage easels. Hire beats buying for anything you'll never use again.
ask_suppliers: Delivery/collection included?
What's the damage deposit?

tasks.title: Vaccinations and travel health
tasks.guidance: Some courses need 6-8 weeks. Check NHS Fit for Travel for your destination.

tasks.title: Take out travel insurance
tasks.guidance: Buy when you book, not when you fly - it covers cancellation from the purchase date.

tasks.title: Check identity documents
tasks.guidance: For giving notice you'll need passport, proof of address, and decree absolute if divorced (or previous partner's death certificate if widowed). Foreign nationals may need extra documentation - check GOV.UK early.

tasks.title: Ask your readers
tasks.guidance: Give readers their text well in advance and tell them roughly when in the ceremony they're on. A printed copy on the day saves phone-fumbling.

tasks.title: Apostille and translate your documents
tasks.guidance: Most countries require the CNI (and sometimes birth certificates) legalised with an FCDO apostille, then officially translated. Allow 6-8 weeks for the full chain. Your local planner will confirm exactly what the registrar there wants.

tasks.title: Plan the order of the day
tasks.guidance: Ceremony time, drinks reception, wedding breakfast, speeches, first dance, evening guests arriving, carriages. Your venue coordinator will help - typical UK pattern: 1pm ceremony, 3pm speeches or after meal, 7.30pm evening do.

tasks.title: Give formal notice of marriage
tasks.guidance: Legal requirement for civil ceremonies: give notice at your LOCAL register office at least 29 days before the wedding (you must have lived in the district 7 days). £35 per person. The resulting marriage schedule is valid 12 months. Book the appointment early - slots fill up.

tasks.title: First dress fitting
tasks.guidance: Bring your actual shoes and underwear. Typical alteration cost £100-£300 - budget for it, it's rarely included.

tasks.title: Book evening food
tasks.guidance: Evening guests plus dancing = hungry crowd around 9pm. Bacon rolls, pizza van or grazing table; cater for ~75% of total evening numbers.

tasks.title: Finalise flower selections
tasks.guidance: Confirm final counts (buttonholes, corsages, table arrangements) once RSVP numbers and the table count are near-final.

tasks.title: Choose your first dance
tasks.guidance: Pick something that means something - and check the length; four minutes of swaying feels long. A couple of dance lessons (£80-£150) buys confidence, not choreography.

tasks.title: Check UK recognition of your marriage
tasks.guidance: A marriage legally performed abroad is generally recognised in the UK without re-registering - but keep the foreign certificate safe and get a certified translation. You can deposit it with the GRO for safekeeping.

tasks.title: Plan how to pay suppliers abroad
tasks.guidance: Use a fee-free multi-currency card or account for transfers - bank international payment fees add up across 6-8 suppliers. Confirm whether quotes include local taxes.

tasks.title: Plan guest welcome events
tasks.guidance: Guests travelled a long way for you - a welcome drinks evening and one group activity turns the wedding into a holiday. Keep other days free; nobody wants a compulsory itinerary.

tasks.title: Have banns read (Church of England)
tasks.guidance: Only for CofE weddings: banns are read in your parish church(es) on three Sundays during the three months before the wedding. Your vicar arranges this - attend at least one reading.

tasks.title: Plan children's entertainment
tasks.guidance: If children are coming: activity packs, a movie corner, or a creche service (£200-£400) buys their parents a proper evening.

tasks.title: Make DIY décor
tasks.guidance: Be honest about time: 10 centrepieces takes a weekend, not an evening. Finish a full sample first, then batch. Recruit help.

tasks.title: Hair and makeup trial
tasks.guidance: Do it on a day with something on afterwards to test how it lasts. Photograph the result in daylight. Take your veil and hair accessories.

tasks.title: Write your vows
tasks.guidance: If personalising: 1-2 minutes each is plenty, agree tone together (funny vs tearful), and don't share them before the day. Legal declarations still apply in civil ceremonies.

tasks.title: Track and chase RSVPs
tasks.guidance: Chase stragglers the week after your deadline - there are always some. Collect dietary requirements as replies come in, not after.

tasks.title: Share guest travel and parking info
tasks.guidance: Postcode quirks, parking, taxi numbers for the end of the night, shuttle times if you're running one. A simple info page saves fifty identical questions.

tasks.title: Order on-the-day stationery
tasks.guidance: Menus, place cards, table numbers, table plan, order of service. Needs near-final RSVPs and menu, so don't order early.

tasks.title: Order signage
tasks.guidance: Welcome sign, order-of-the-day sign, bar menu. Nice-to-have; a good calligrapher friend or printable template does the job for a fraction of the cost.

tasks.title: Write the speeches
tasks.guidance: 5-7 minutes maximum each. Thank people, one or two stories, a toast - done. Practise out loud at least twice. Nudge the best man and father of the bride too.

tasks.title: Final venue details meeting
tasks.guidance: Walk through the full day with your coordinator: timings, room layouts, supplier access times, where gifts and cards live, who's your point of contact on the day.

tasks.title: Suit fittings
tasks.guidance: Everyone tries everything on - shoes too. Check sleeve and trouser lengths; hire shops fix issues in days if you flag them now.

tasks.title: Agree the shot list and family groups
tasks.guidance: Keep formal group shots to 6-8 (each takes ~5 minutes of your drinks reception). Nominate a bossy relative to gather people.

tasks.title: Send playlists and do-not-play list
tasks.guidance: Must-plays (10-15 songs), do-not-plays, first dance version, and the last song of the night. Then trust the professional to read the room.

tasks.title: Build the day-of run sheet
tasks.guidance: Minute-by-minute from hair and makeup to carriages: who, where, when, contact number. Share with venue, suppliers and wedding party. Luxe tier: Buzz generates this for you.

tasks.title: Plan the dress and suit journey
tasks.guidance: Never check the dress into the hold. Call the airline in advance - many allow it as carry-on in a garment bag or cabin wardrobe. Arrange local steaming on arrival; your planner will know who.

tasks.title: Build the seating plan
tasks.guidance: Start with the top table, then group by friendship not obligation. Keep known feuds apart. Expect to redo it at least twice as late RSVPs land.

tasks.title: Break in your wedding shoes
tasks.guidance: Wear them around the house for an hour at a time. Your feet will thank you at hour ten of the day.

tasks.title: Confirm every supplier in writing
tasks.guidance: One email each: date, arrival time, venue address, on-the-day contact (NOT you - a wedding party member), and outstanding balance. Keep the replies.

tasks.title: Delegate day-of roles
tasks.guidance: Someone owns: supplier phone calls, gathering people for photos, gifts and cards security, taking décor home. You and your partner should own nothing on the day.

tasks.title: Buy wedding-party and parent gifts
tasks.guidance: Traditionally given at the rehearsal dinner or during speeches. Personal beats expensive.

tasks.title: Final dress fitting and collection
tasks.guidance: Take whoever's helping you dress on the day so they learn the fastenings. Arrange safe storage - hanging, in a breathable bag, away from curious eyes.

tasks.title: Send the final dietary matrix
tasks.guidance: Every allergy and requirement against a named guest and table number, sent in writing. Confetti compiles this from your RSVPs automatically.

tasks.title: Send the day plan to photo and video
tasks.guidance: Timings, both getting-ready addresses, supplier contact numbers, sunset time if you want golden-hour shots.

tasks.title: Confirm final numbers to the venue
tasks.guidance: Day and evening counts, dietary matrix, and table plan. This usually fixes your final invoice.

tasks.title: Order thank-you cards
tasks.guidance: Order before the wedding (matching your suite) so they're ready when you're back from honeymoon. Photo cards need to wait for photos - plain ones don't.

tasks.title: Confirm setup and delivery times
tasks.guidance: Florist and décor suppliers need venue access times, a named contact, and to know who takes everything down. Put it all in one email.

tasks.title: Pre-wedding haircuts and colour
tasks.guidance: 1-2 weeks before is the sweet spot - fresh but settled. Both partners.

tasks.title: Confirm all pickup times and routes
tasks.guidance: Written confirmation: addresses, times, contact numbers both ways. Add 15 minutes of slack to every journey.

tasks.title: Ceremony rehearsal
tasks.guidance: Walk the entrance order, positions and exit with the wedding party. CofE weddings usually rehearse with the vicar; civil venues often do a quick run-through at the details meeting.

tasks.title: Nails and final grooming
tasks.guidance: 2-3 days before. Gel lasts the honeymoon. Book the wedding party in together and make an event of it.

tasks.title: Pack and sort currency
tasks.guidance: Pack BEFORE the wedding week chaos. A fee-free travel card beats bureau de change rates.

tasks.title: Prepare final payments and tips
tasks.guidance: Pay balances due before the day by transfer; put any on-the-day cash in labelled envelopes with the best man. Tipping isn't obligatory in the UK but is appreciated for great service.

tasks.title: Assemble a wedding-day emergency kit
tasks.guidance: Safety pins, fashion tape, sewing kit, plasters, painkillers, stain wipes, phone chargers, snacks, tissues, spare tights, deodorant. Give it to a bridesmaid.

tasks.title: Print everything and pack the venue box
tasks.guidance: Place cards, table plan, menus, guest book and pens, cake knife if needed, card box. One labelled crate, delivered at the venue meeting or by a delegate.

tasks.title: Hand over rings and documents
tasks.guidance: Rings to the best man. For civil ceremonies your marriage schedule must be at the ceremony - confirm who's bringing it (usually the venue or registrar holds it; check).

tasks.title: Rehearsal and pre-wedding dinner
tasks.guidance: A relaxed dinner with the wedding party the night before settles everyone. Keep it early and light on the wine.

tasks.title: Enjoy the day
tasks.guidance: Eat breakfast. Hydrate. Take one quiet moment together during the evening to stand back and watch the party you built. Everything else is someone else's job today.

tasks.title: Return hired items
tasks.guidance: Suits, décor, cake stands - most hire terms are 2-3 days after the event before charges. Delegate this before the honeymoon.

tasks.title: Settle final invoices
tasks.guidance: Check each invoice against the quote and contract before paying. Query discrepancies politely and in writing.

tasks.title: Order marriage certificate copies
tasks.guidance: £12.50 per copy from the register office. Get 2-3 - banks and passport applications often want originals.

tasks.title: Handle name-change admin
tasks.guidance: If changing: passport, driving licence, bank, HMRC, employer, GP, utilities. The marriage certificate is your evidence - no deed poll needed for taking a spouse's surname.

tasks.title: Review your suppliers
tasks.guidance: Reviews are how good wedding suppliers survive - five minutes each repays people who made your day.

tasks.title: Clean and preserve the dress
tasks.guidance: Stains set with time - specialist cleaning within a month is best. Preservation boxing costs £100-£200 if you're keeping it; selling pre-loved is increasingly normal.

tasks.title: Send thank-you cards
tasks.guidance: Within three months is the convention. Mention the specific gift - Confetti's gift log per guest makes this painless.

tasks.title: Receive and share your photos
tasks.guidance: Galleries typically arrive 4-8 weeks after the day. Back them up in two places before sharing the link.
